comments.text: On a related question - At the end of [DEGS77], there are a number of open questions asked. One is about the value of $$\alpha=\sup_{A\in\mathcal{A}} \liminf_{n\to\infty} \frac{|A\cap[1,n]|}{n}$$ and $$\beta=\sup_{A\in\mathcal{A}} \limsup_{n\to\infty} \frac{|A\cap[1,n]|}{n} $$ where $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all infinite subsets, $A$, of $\mathbb{Z}^+$ for which there exists a permutation of $A$ that does not contain a monotone $3$-term arithmetic progression. In On Permutations Avoiding Arithmetic Progressions, the authors show that $\alpha\geq 1/2$ and $\beta\geq 1/4$ by constructing sets $A$ that have these upper and lower densities. They conjecture that these bounds are tight. Currently, there is no known method of finding non-trivial upper bounds for $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

The current statement "Must every permutation of $\mathbb{N}$ contain a monotone $4$-term arithmetic progression $x_1 < x_2 < x_3 < x_4$" seems to cause some confusion, see https://leanprover.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/524981-Formal-conjectures/topic/Erd.C5.91s.20196/near/537227537 https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/pull/645#issuecomment-3247874428 Is it correct that the intention here is as follows? "Must every permutation of $\mathbb{N}$ contain a monotone $4$-term arithmetic progression, i.e. given a permutation $x$, there are indices $i, j, k, l$ such that either $i< j< k <l$ or $i > j > k > l$, and $a_i, a_j, a_k, a_l$ is an arithmetic progression (in that order)." (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

From p.337 of [ErGr79]: "Let us say that a sequence $(a_1,a_2,\dots)$ has a monotone AP of length $k$ if for some choice of indices $i_1 < i_2 < \dots < i_k$, the subsequence $a_{i_1}, a_{i_2}, \dots, a_{i_k}$ is either an increasing or decreasing AP". From this reading I believe your formulation is the intended one (especially given the ensuing discussion describing finite sets of integers with no monotone 3-APs, where a key point being that the first and last elements of a 3-AP have the same parity, suggesting that rearrangements of an AP that are not in increasing or decreasing order are not considered).
